title: Dead of Winter: A Crossroads Game

component_identification.content: Using the official rulebook text as the primary source, set up Dead Of Winter by first sorting all components into player materials, shared components, randomizers, and state markers. Verify component names and iconography against the rulebook contents section before anyone chooses a role or starts the first turn. This avoids the most common setup failure: missing or mis-sorted decks/tokens that break later rules interactions. The rulebook references these core pieces repeatedly in setup and play: COMPONENTS • 10 – Dual-Sided Main Objective Cards • 24 – Secret Objective Cards • 10 – Betrayal Secret Objective Cards • 10 – Exiled Secret Objective Cards • 30 – Survivor Cards • 5 – Player Reference Sheets • 1 – First Player Token • 25 – Starting Item Cards • 20 – Police Station Item Deck Cards • 20 – Grocery Store Item Deck Cards • 20 – School Item Deck Cards • 20 – Gas Station Item Deck Cards • 20 – Library Item Deck Cards • 20 – Hospital Item Deck ...

core_game_loop.content: The official rules describe a repeatable turn rhythm for Dead Of Winter: perform start-of-turn checks, execute the game’s main action structure in the stated order, resolve triggered effects, then complete cleanup/end-step timing before passing play. Teach this as a fixed checklist so new players do not skip mandatory windows. If an interrupt or reaction occurs, resolve it completely, then return to the unresolved step. Rulebook turn-sequence cues include: Player Turns Library PLAYER TURN ACTIONS GROCERY STORE ACTIONS THAT REQUIRE AN ACTION DIE PLAYER TURNS PHASE ROUND SUMMARY GROUP LEADER PLAYER TURNS PHASE 1. Pass First Player Token COLONY PHASE 2 FOLLOWERS 11 • { Attack (zombie or survivor) • } Search • Barricade • Clean Waste (3 cards) • Attract (2 zombies) • Play a Card • Add a Card to the Crisis • Move a Survivor • Spend Food Tokens • Request • Hand Off • Vote to Exile ...

detailed_rules.content: Detailed play should follow a strict rulebook hierarchy: explicit card/effect text first, scenario-specific instructions second, and baseline turn structure third. In Dead Of Winter, most disputes come from timing windows, target legality, replacement effects, and whether an action is optional or mandatory. Read 'must', 'may', and 'cannot' literally; these terms define obligations and restrictions. Always verify eligibility before paying costs, then resolve effects sentence by sentence. If an effect changes board state mid-resolution, finish the current instruction chain before opening new optional actions unless the text says otherwise. Important operational clauses extracted from the official rulebook include: When the player has decided he has made enough noise or can no longer make any more noise, he must keep 1 of the drawn cards, adding it to his hand, and place the rest 9 GAS STATION Example: search a location 1 GAS STATION FOREST PLUM • Barricade: To perform a barricade action, a player must choose a survivor he controls and spend an action die of any result to place a barricade token on any empty entrance space at the chosen survivor’s location. We’ve got armed cannibals in our backyard.” OPTION 1 1 3 BLIZZARD 3 OPTION 2 2 += number of non-exiled players For the remainder of the game, whenever any player moves a survivor, in addition to rolling the exposure die they must roll a regular die. Whenever a zombie is killed by an attack or card effect, the player that controls the survivor that killed that zombie must roll for exposure on that survivor. Note: If the result ...

scoring_and_endgame.content: Scoring and endgame are determined only by printed rulebook conditions. During play, track all score/objective progress continuously so players can identify whether the game ends immediately on trigger or after round completion. When an end trigger occurs, resolve pending mandatory effects in the order defined by the rules, then apply final scoring and tie-breakers exactly as written. beginner_strategy.content: Beginner strategy from the official rules for Dead Of Winter: (1) Prioritize legal, repeatable value every turn before attempting high-variance combos. (2) Plan around timing windows—many beginner mistakes are not tactical, they are sequencing errors. (3) Preserve flexibility by keeping at least one adaptable action/resource each round, so you can respond to interrupts and shifting board states. (4) Study loss conditions and penalties early; defensive rule awareness prevents avoidable collapses. (5) In the endgame, choose objective-completing actions over cosmetic optimization, because trigger timing usually matters more than one extra short-term gain. These recommendations come directly from the turn structure and win-condition logic in the official rulebook text.
